content.problemStatement:
Pesla needs to charge NNN cars before sending them to space. The ithi^{th}ith car has an energy capacity AiA_iAi​ Watt-hours.

There are MMM power outlets. The jthj^{th}jth outlet provides a power of BjB_jBj​ Watt.

If an outlet can charge **at most** one car and a car can be charged by **at most** one outlet, find the **maximum** total energy (in Watt-hours) stored in all cars after HHH hours.

Note:


A power outlet **cannot** charge a different car even after completely charging a car.
Energy is the product of power and time. For instance, a car can store 111 Watt-hour of energy if it is charged at a power station with 111 Watt power for 111 hour.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains 333 space-separated integers NNN, MMM, and HHH, the number of cars, the number of power outlets, and the number of hours respectively.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers, the energy capacities (in Watt-hour) of the NNN cars.
The third line of each test case contains MMM space-separated integers, the power (in Watt) of the MMM power outlets.


Output Format
For each test case, print the maximum total energy (in Watt-hours) stored in all cars after HHH hours.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains three space‑separated integers N, M and H — the number of cars, the number of power outlets and the charging time in hours. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN — the energy capacities (in Watt‑hours) of the cars. The third line contains M space‑separated integers B1 … BM — the power (in Watt) of each outlet.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum total energy (in Watt‑hours) that can be stored in the cars after H hours.

solution.approach: Each outlet can deliver a fixed amount of energy equal to Bi·H. A car can store at most its capacity Ai. The problem reduces to pairing cars with outlets to maximise the sum of min(Ai, Bi·H). The optimal strategy is to match the largest capacities with the largest available energies. Sort the car capacities in non‑increasing order and the outlet energies (Bi·H) also in non‑increasing order, then pair them one‑by‑one up to min(N, M) and accumulate min of each pair. This greedy pairing is optimal because any deviation would replace a larger value with a smaller one, never increasing the total.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N+M) log (N+M)) per test case due to sorting; overall O(∑(N+M) log (N+M)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N+M) for the two arrays per test case.
